events.name: Quadrantid Meteor Shower
events.description: One of the best meteor showers of the year with a sharp peak, producing up to 120 meteors per hour. The Quadrantids are known for their bright fireballs and can produce spectacular displays during optimal conditions. Best viewed in the early morning hours before dawn.
events.historicalNotes: Named after the constellation Quadrans Muralis, which is no longer recognized.

events.name: Lyrid Meteor Shower
events.description: Medium-strength shower that occasionally produces outbursts of up to 100 meteors per hour. The Lyrids are one of the oldest recorded meteor showers, with observations dating back over 2,700 years. Known for occasional bright fireballs and persistent trains.
events.historicalNotes: One of the oldest recorded meteor showers, observed by Chinese astronomers in 687 BC.

events.name: Eta Aquariid Meteor Shower
events.description: Excellent shower for southern hemisphere viewers with fast, bright meteors. The Eta Aquariids are known for their high speed and can produce spectacular fireballs. Best viewed in the pre-dawn hours when the radiant is highest in the sky.
events.historicalNotes: Debris from Halley's Comet, visible every year when Earth passes through its trail.

events.name: Perseid Meteor Shower
events.description: One of the best meteor showers of the year, producing up to 100 meteors per hour at its peak. The Perseids are famous for their consistent performance and spectacular fireballs. Ideal for beginners as they occur during warm summer nights in the Northern Hemisphere.
events.historicalNotes: Associated with Comet Swift-Tuttle, which has a 133-year orbit.

events.name: Draconid Meteor Shower
events.description: Unpredictable shower that can produce meteor storms with hundreds of meteors per hour. The Draconids are unique as they're best viewed in the evening rather than early morning. While typically weak, they can produce spectacular outbursts during certain years.
events.historicalNotes: Produced spectacular meteor storms in 1933 and 1946 with thousands of meteors per hour.

events.name: Orionid Meteor Shower
events.description: Medium-strength shower with fast meteors, debris from Halley's Comet. The Orionids are known for their speed and can produce bright fireballs with persistent trains. Best viewed after midnight when the radiant is high in the sky.
events.historicalNotes: Second meteor shower from Halley's Comet, along with the Eta Aquariids.

events.name: Taurid Meteor Shower
events.description: Known for bright fireballs and slow-moving meteors, split into Northern and Southern streams. The Taurids are famous for producing exceptionally bright fireballs that can light up the entire sky. Though rates are low, the fireballs make this shower memorable.
events.historicalNotes: Debris from Comet Encke, the shortest-period comet known (3.3 years).

events.name: Leonid Meteor Shower
events.description: Famous for producing meteor storms every 33 years, with rates reaching thousands per hour. The Leonids are known for their fast meteors and can produce spectacular fireballs. While most years show modest activity, storm years can be unforgettable.
events.historicalNotes: Produced spectacular storms in 1966 and 2001 with thousands of meteors per hour.

events.name: Geminid Meteor Shower
events.description: One of the best and most reliable meteor showers, producing up to 120 multicolored meteors per hour. The Geminids are known for their consistent performance and colorful meteors. Unlike most showers, they originate from an asteroid rather than a comet.
events.historicalNotes: Unique meteor shower originating from an asteroid rather than a comet.

events.name: Ursid Meteor Shower
events.description: Minor shower that occasionally produces outbursts, visible from northern latitudes. The Ursids peak near the winter solstice and can sometimes surprise observers with enhanced activity. Best viewed in the early morning hours before dawn.
events.bestViewing: After midnight, looking toward Ursa Minor
events.historicalNotes: Often overlooked due to proximity to Geminids, but can produce surprises.

events.name: Southern Delta Aquariid Meteor Shower
events.description: Reliable summer shower with faint meteors, best viewed from southern latitudes.
events.historicalNotes: One of two Delta Aquariid showers, along with the Northern stream.

events.name: Total Solar Eclipse
events.description: A spectacular total solar eclipse visible across North America.
events.bestViewing: Path of totality from Mexico to Canada

events.name: Supermoon
events.description: When the Moon is at its closest point to Earth, appearing larger and brighter.

events.name: Saturn at Opposition
events.description: Saturn is at its closest approach to Earth and fully illuminated by the Sun.

events.name: Jupiter at Opposition
events.description: Jupiter is at its closest approach to Earth and fully illuminated by the Sun.

events.name: Total Lunar Eclipse
events.description: The Moon passes through Earth's shadow, often appearing reddish (Blood Moon).
